claimed_contribution: The paper introduces and tests fitness branding in food marketing as a factor that can affect both food consumption and postconsumption physical activity.
It argues that fitness cues can increase restrained eaters' consumption similarly to diet cues, but that fitness branding has a unique effect of reducing later physical activity.
It claims to extend goal-conflict and compensatory-belief perspectives by showing that eating fitness-branded food can function as a substitute for actual exercise among restrained eaters.
context: The paper studies food marketing settings in which product branding and packaging include fitness-related cues (e.g., the label "Fitness" and athletic imagery). Evidence comes from controlled university laboratory consumption tasks using trail mix, with physical activity measured after eating in one study.
design_and_data: The authors report three between-subjects lab experiments with university students. Study 1 (n = 162 after one exclusion) compared fitness-labeled versus unlabeled trail mix and tested calorie intake as a function of dietary restraint (Dutch Eating Behavior Questionnaire). Study 2 (n = 230 after one exclusion) used a 2 x 2 design crossing fitness label (vs no label) with framing of the same food as dietary permitted versus dietary forbidden, with intake as the outcome. Study 3 (n = 144 after one exclusion) compared fitness label, diet label, and no label under permitted framing, measured both intake and energy expenditure on an isokinetic cycle ergometer, and tested moderated mediation with intake as mediator.
focal_constructs: dietary permitted versus dietary forbidden framing
main_findings: Fitness branding increased calorie consumption for restrained eaters relative to no fitness branding.
The positive consumption effect of fitness branding occurred when food was framed as dietary permitted and disappeared when food was framed as dietary forbidden.
After consuming fitness-branded food, restrained eaters expended less energy on physical activity than after diet-branded or nonbranded food, and increased intake mediated this effect.
one_line_summary: Across three laboratory studies, fitness branding on food increased restrained eaters' intake of dietary-permitted food and reduced their subsequent physical activity, with intake mediating the activity reduction.
relation_to_prior_work: extends prior work on diet and health cues by showing that integral fitness branding on food can also increase consumption among restrained eaters. It also extends goal-conflict and compensatory-belief accounts by linking fitness-branded intake to lower subsequent physical activity.
research_gap: Prior research had mostly examined incidental fitness cues and simple main effects on consumption, leaving the effects of integral fitness branding in food marketing underexplored. The authors also identify limited evidence on downstream physical-activity consequences of fitness-branded food and on how these effects compare with diet branding.
research_question: The paper asks how fitness branding in food marketing influences restrained versus unrestrained eaters' food consumption and postconsumption physical activity. It further examines whether consumption effects depend on whether food is perceived as dietary permitted or dietary forbidden, and whether any physical-activity effect is unique to fitness branding relative to diet branding.
